FYI.  [REDACTED] from today's CPUC meeting.  It is still in progress,
with few energy items remaining.  Regular results memo to follow.

CPUC opens new rulemaking to return to "integrated resource planning"
with contemplation of process that sounds much like the discredited
[REDACTED] (BRPU) proceeding.  Clearly contemplates
utility construction of new generation projects, but also calls for
life-cycle cost analysis of all options, including new asset
construction, power purchase agreements, conservation, transmission,
etc.

CPUC rejects new interstate transmission facilities as not needed for
reliability purposes (lines to NV, AZ and MX).  Will begin new phase re
economic projects.

CPUC rejects request for clarification regarding QF contract
amendments.  Parties had requested clarification from CPUC decision
based on language in [REDACTED] (ALJ) ruling that stated extensions
could go forward until the Commission acts.  CPUC states that parties
remain free to negotiation changes, but that they must move forward on
ordinary review process.  This decision primarily undermines QF contract
amendments with SCE's.

CPUC rejects PG&E's call for market valuation of retained generation.
PG&E argued that CPUC must use market value of retained assets to set
cost of service rates.  CPUC rejects argument.  Duque will concur, but
notes that there are outstanding issues including the likelihood that
CPUC will fail to meet statutory deadline to market value retained
assets, and fact that PG&E still has stranded cost recovery rights that
require market valuation.  It is also unclear, notwithstanding the CPUC
settlement with SCE, how post-transitions period costs will be recovered
per that agreement.

Here is a continuation of the text:

It appears that the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) is taking steps to revisit the concept of integrated resource planning, which was previously discredited. The new rulemaking proceeding aims to consider a range of options, including utility construction of new generation projects, power purchase agreements, conservation, transmission, and other alternatives. This move is likely to have significant implications for the state's energy market and may impact the contracts signed by the California Department of Water Resources (CDWR).

One of the key issues at play is the role of competitive markets in California's energy sector. The CPUC's decision to revisit integrated resource planning suggests that the state may be moving away from a purely market-based approach. This could have significant implications for companies like Enron, which had been actively involved in California's energy market.

As the email chain suggests, there are many stakeholders who are watching this development closely. The CPUC's decision to reject new interstate transmission facilities and PG&E's call for market valuation of retained generation are also likely to be significant. The fact that the CPUC is contemplating a return to more traditional utility planning and construction of new generation projects raises questions about the future of competitive markets in California.

As we continue to monitor the developments regarding the California Department of Water Resources' (DWR) proposed revenue requirement, Southern California Edison (SCE) has forwarded a series of questions to DWR. These inquiries are aimed at clarifying certain aspects of DWR's proposal that SCE believes need further explanation or justification.

The queries cover several key areas, including the methodology used to calculate the proposed revenue requirement, the underlying assumptions regarding future energy demand and prices, and the impact of recent legislative changes on DWR's financing costs.

SCE has requested that DWR provide detailed responses to these questions to facilitate a more comprehensive understanding of the proposal among stakeholders. We understand that DWR is working on compiling the necessary information and will respond to SCE's inquiries in due course.

We will continue to keep you updated on any significant developments in this matter.

Best

Subject: RE: SDG&E's July 16th Filing with the CPUC

Dear All,

On July 16th, SDG&E filed a Motion requesting certain actions from the Commission necessary to implement its Memorandum of Understanding with DWR. There are ten specific actions provided for in the MOU. The motion either requests that the action occur or references another CPUC proceeding where the matter is pending.

Please let me know if Enron is interested in pursuing any matters related to the SDG&E filing. We would appreciate your feedback by the end of the week so that we can coordinate our response.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at

on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction

against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our

now.

Here is a continuation of the given context:

In light of the concerns raised, it is likely that the parties will need to revisit the draft order and make some adjustments. The suggestion to define "ripple claims" upfront is a good one, as it will help to avoid any confusion or misunderstandings. The proposed definition of a "ripple claim" as any refund claim that could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed opposition to refunds in this case is a reasonable one.

The issue of whether to defer or exclude ripple claims will also need to be addressed. While the draft order currently defers ripple claims to a later phase, some parties may argue that they should be excluded altogether. The argument that the proceeding should not be allowed to "live on in later phases" is a persuasive one, as it is generally desirable to resolve all issues in a single proceeding if possible.

In terms of next steps, it is likely that the parties will need to engage in further discussions to resolve these issues. This may involve a conference call or an in-person meeting to discuss the concerns and suggestions that have been raised. The goal will be to reach a consensus on the definition of "ripple claims" and the treatment of such claims in the proceeding.

Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our

now.

"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

Dear All,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to follow up on the draft order regarding ripple claims that was circulated earlier. As I mentioned during the prehearing conference, I have some concerns that I would like to bring to the attention of the group.

As you know, the draft order proposes to defer consideration of ripple claims to a later phase in this proceeding. I understand the reasoning behind this approach, but I have some concerns that I believe need to be addressed before we move forward.

While the judge indicated that she would accept a party's "ballpark estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for ripple claim offers of proof could potentially harm a party that remains silent on this issue.

"Re: Draft Order on Ripple Claims

Dear All,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to follow up on the draft order regarding ripple claims that was circulated earlier. As you may recall, I had some concerns regarding the language used in the draft and I wanted to bring them to your attention.

While the judge did indicate that she would accept a party's "ballpark estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for offers of proof could potentially harm a party that remains silent on this issue.

In addition, I think it would be beneficial to define ripple claims upfront in the order. To do this, we need to ensure that we are all in agreement on the meaning of the terms we are using. Depending on how we define ripple claims, it's possible that a counterclaim or cross-claim could be considered a ripple claim, and we need to be cautious not to prejudice ourselves by failing to assert such claims now.

As I understand it, a counterclaim is a claim made against a party that has already asserted a claim against us, while a cross

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?
